title: The PRIDE Study
text:
Lab Manual
The PRIDE Study at Stanford University is the first large-scale, long-term, community-engaged national health study of people who identify as lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer, intersex, asexual, or another sexual or gender minority (LGBTQIA+). The PRIDE team an interdisciplinary group of clinicians, scientists, and research staff who are dedicated to addressing critical social injustices and health challenges facing LGBTQIA+ populations. The co-directors of The PRIDE Study are:

Dr. Annesa Flentje, PhD
Dr. Mitchell R. Lunn, MD, MAS
Dr. Juno Obedin-Maliver, MD, MPH, MAS

This lab manual serves 4 purposes: 1) articulating our lab culture and philosophy, 2) outlining general policies that support our daily work, 3) documenting the design of The PRIDE Study, and 4) offering reference materials for reproducible research. The manual will evolve over time through contributions from lab members. Please send feedback to Nguyen Tran or submit a pull request on our GitHub repository.
The project was supported by the National Center for Advancing Translational Sciences of the National Institutes of Health under Award Number UM1TR004921. The content is solely the responsibility of the authors and does not necessarily represent the official views of the National Institutes of Health.
Development of this manual was inspired by the lab manual from the Stanford Health Policy Data Science and the Kiang Lab.

title: 1  Lab Culture
text:
1.1 Community Engagement
PRIDE is committed to advancing health equity and improving the lives of LGBTQIA+ communities through rigorous, community-engaged research. We leverage data to generate evidence that informs public health interventions, shapes policy, and addresses the critical social injustices and health inequities facing LGBTQIA+ populations. We believe that transformative research emerges from a lab culture grounded in curiosity, collaboration, kindness, and inclusion. We maintain high standards for scientific rigor, quality, and ethical integrity, and we support one another in reaching these goals through mentorship, open dialogue, and shared learning.
Every lab member is valued first and foremost as a whole person. We are committed to understanding and supporting each individual’s unique career aspirations, personal circumstances, and professional development goals. We recognize that our best work happens when people feel safe, respected, and empowered to bring their full selves to the research process. We are committed to an environment that is free from discrimination and harassment.
Our work is grounded in a commitment to community partnerships and social justice. We recognize that meaningful research cannot happen without the communities we serve. We are committed to engaging LGBTQIA+ community members as partners in all stages of research, ensuring our research questions reflect community priorities and needs, translating findings into accessible formats and sharing results with participants and communities, and centering the voices and lived experiences of those most affected by health inequities.

title: 1  Lab Culture
text:
1.2 Collaboration and Mentorship
We believe the most impactful research happens through interdisciplinary collaborations, and we aim to foster collaboration across institutions, disciplines, and community partners while valuing diverse perspectives and expertise. We maintain open and transparent communication through regular lab meetings, one-on-ones, and collaborative platforms, providing constructive feedback with kindness while receiving it with openness. Every lab member is both a teacher and a learner, and we invest in developing technical skills, scientific thinking, and professional competencies while supporting diverse career paths and aspirations. By creating opportunities for mentorship, knowledge exchange, leadership, and independence at all career stages, we build partnerships that strengthen our collective impact. We address conflicts directly and respectfully, and we celebrate successes and support each other through challenges.

title: 1  Lab Culture
text:
1.3 Research Ethics and Data Privacy
We handle participant data with the utmost care and respect for privacy, and we consider the ethical implications of our research questions, methods, and dissemination. All PRIDE Study data is protected under a certificate of confidentiality that prevents investigators from being forced to disclose identifying information in legal, federal, or state proceedings.

title: 2  General Policies
text:
2.1 Communication
This section outlines the operational policies and practical guidelines that support our day-to-day work in PRIDE.
The lab uses multiple platforms for different types of communication, and choosing the appropriate channel helps ensure messages are seen and addressed in a timely manner. Email is usually the preferred for method of communication to team members in most cases. This may include detailed information that requires documentation, or messages involving people outside the lab. For complex discussions, brainstorming, or matters requiring nuanced conversation, we encourage in-person or video meetings rather than lengthy email. For time-sensitive requests or important messages, we ask the lab members that lab members respond within 24 hours.

title: 2  General Policies
text:
2.2 Working Hours
PRIDE trusts that all members of our team can manage their time and work, recognizing that people have different working styles, peak productivity hours, and personal commitments. We prioritize outputs and impact over hours worked, and we support remote work and flexible schedules when feasible.

title: 2  General Policies
text:
2.3 Time Off and Sick Time

2.3.1 Time Off (Vacation, PTO, and Floating Holiday) Requests
PRIDE views time off (vacation, PTO, and floating holiday time) as part of staff’s holistic compensation package, and strongly encourages the use of accrued leave time for well-being and work-life balance. As such, PRIDE’s general philosophy towards requests for using accrued time off is “how can we get to a yes.” This is with the understanding that if there are conflicts, sometimes a “yes” may require adjusting the amount of time taken off or when it is taken.
Time off requests are assessed with the following criteria:

any conflicting project deadlines;
availability for work coverage during the time off;
the employee has enough time accrued.

Time off requests are reviewed in the order they are received. If multiple staff request time off for the same day(s) that would cause a conflict with work coverage, then priority will be provided to the request made first. Longer periods of time off may require the supervisor to first check in with other staff who may be affected.
Any time off must be requested in advance in writing by the employee, which is then assessed for approval or modifications by the supervisor (see Section 2.3.4 for instructions on submitting written requests). Employees are encouraged to not secure any time off plans (e.g., purchasing airfare or hotels) until after their time off request is formally approved.
Below are guidelines for advance notice that employees should provide for their time off requests, and how long to anticipate for the supervisor’s response. Submitting requests within these timeframes does not guarantee approval, but it helps to set the requests up to more easily get to a “yes.”








Amount of Time Off Being Requested by Employee
Amount of Advance Notice Employee Provides
Amount of Time for Supervisor’s Reply




1-2 days off
5+ business days advance notice
3 business days for reply


3-5 days off
10+ business days advance notice
5 business days for reply


6+ days off
20+ business days advance notice
10 business days for reply



PRIDE understands that at times unforeseen situations may arise that make certain requests difficult to adhere to the above guidelines. Employees may still submit requests outside of the above guidelines, with the understanding they may be less likely to be approved if they conflict with work commitments and/or coverage available. PRIDE also understands that emergency situations may arise without advance notice, in which case employees are encouraged to consult with their supervisor directly.
Please read full details about vacation, PTO, and floating holiday accruals and institution-wide policies at Stanford.


2.3.2 Flex Time and Schedule Adjustment Requests
PRIDE’s work sometimes includes required events, conferences, and/or formal activities over the weekend. In these cases, flex time or schedule adjustments should be utilized.
Non-exempt employees will request in writing a schedule adjustment for the weekday(s) prior to the weekend worked, unless overtime is pre-approved by the supervisor. Overtime is defined as working more than 8 hours in one day or more than 40 hours in one week (for only the purpose of calculating overtime, Stanford’s work week is Monday through Sunday). At times a supervisor may assign a schedule adjustment to a non-exempt employee based on required weekend work assignments, in which case best efforts will be used to provide the employee with at least 10 business days advance notice.

An employee’s schedule adjustment request should mirror the number of hours worked on the weekend. For example, if someone works 4 hours at an event on a Saturday, then the schedule adjustment request should be 4 hours of not working on a Monday through Friday of the week preceding that Saturday worked.

Exempt employees will request in writing flex time, which should be requested within 10 business days of the weekend work (the flex time does not need to be used within 10 business days, but the request needs to be submitted by then).

Flex time requests should mirror the amount of time worked on the weekend in increments of a half-day (for working between 1-4 hours) or a full day (for working between 5-8 hours).
Flex time for exempt employees is limited to weekend work for events, conferences, or formal activities (e.g., facilitating a community listening session). If exempt staff elect to spend time on the weekend for their day-to-day work, this is excluded from flex time unless it is a special circumstance that is discussed and pre-approved by the supervisor.

See Section 2.3.4 Documentation Process Checklist for instructions on submitting written requests.


2.3.3 Sick Time Notification / Requests
Sick time is most frequently not requested with advance notice, as employees can’t predict when they will get sick. Some sick time is possible with advance notice, for example medical procedures or medical appointments. See Section 2.3.4 below for the appropriate processes for employees to notify their supervisors of sick time with advance notice and sick time without advance notice.
Please read full details about sick time accruals, eligible situations to use sick time, and institution-wide policies at Stanford.


2.3.4 Documentation Process Checklist
Time Off, Flex Time, Schedule Adjustments, and Sick Time with Advance Notice

Employee submits the request on their individual Asana project “<<initial’s>> Time Off Requests & Sick Leave.” See sample Asana project for the proper details.
If approved by the supervisor:

Employee adds the time off to the PRIDE team calendar
Employee blocks out the time on their personal Outlook calendar
Employee sets up their out of office email reply for the time off, including: 1) when they will return; 2) who to reach out to with anything time sensitive
Employee plans for work coverage while they are out, in coordination with their supervisor
If the employee will be out of office for 6+ days, send a heads-up emails to the PRIDE team at least 5 business days in advance
Employee documents on their timecard prior to their time off
When the employee returns from time off: 1) Turn off out of office email reply, 2) Ensure their timecard accurately reflected their time off


Sick Time Without Advance Notice

Employee notifies their supervisor through email, call, or text before their work day starts and as soon as they can
Employee determines with their supervisor who will add this to the PRIDE team calendar
If possible, employee sets up their out of office email reply, including who to reach out to with anything time sensitive
When the employee returns: 1) Turn off out of office email reply, 2) Document on: a) Asana (retroactively), b) timecard

title: 3  Publications and Conferences
text:
3.1 Authorship
We aim to discuss authorship early in the research process and have continuing conversations regarding team member roles. Plans can change and contributions may evolve over time. If you have authorship questions during the process of working on a project, we want you to feel empowered to ask these questions. There are many possible guidelines for determining authorship. PRIDE recommends ICMJE recommendations guidelines as one possible for determining who gets authorship and in what order:
Beyond ICMJE, PRIDE suggests using Contributor Role Taxonomy (CRediT) as an option for outlining the responsibilities of each author. It is recommended that CRediT statements are included in the acknowledgements of manuscripts to report each author’s contribution. This clarifies roles and is increasingly requested by journals.
For all papers or conference presentations using PRIDE data, it is expected that one of the co-Directors will serve as the last author.
While not required, we encourage investigators using PRIDE data to include community partners as co-authors on manuscripts when appropriate. Our PRIDEnet community partners provide valuable feedback throughout the research process and often make substantial contributions to study design, interpretation, and dissemination that merit authorship recognition.

title: 3  Publications and Conferences
text:
Substantial contributions to conception or design OR data acquisition, analysis, or interpretation
AND drafting the article or revising it critically for important intellectual content
AND final approval and agreement to be accountable for the work

title: 3  Publications and Conferences
text:
3.2 Publications
Scientific writing is difficult! For anyone new to scientific writing (or those who have been writing for a long time), PRIDE recommends referring to reporting guidelines published as part of the EQUATOR network (Enhancing the QUAlity and Transparency Of health Research)—an international initiative that seeks to improve the reliability and value of published health research literature. Reporting guidelines promote clear reporting of methods and results to allow critical appraisal of the manuscript. The EQUATOR networks has several reporting guidelines for different types of study design, and for many biomedical and health journals, these guidelines are sometimes asked to be submitted as supplemental files by journals along with the main manuscript.
Given the study design of The PRIDE Study, the 2 most common reporting guidelines are:

Strengthening the reporting of observational studies in epidemiology (STROBE)
Consolidated criteria for reporting qualitative research (COREQ)

All manuscripts using data from The PRIDE Study are required to be reviewed by The PRIDE Study prior to submission for publication. This is a necessary step to confirm that the submissions are aligned with The PRIDE Study’s mission and match the approved Ancillary Study application. Collaborators (internal or external to PRIDE) should submit their complete manuscript and all associated files for review through our online submission form.
PRIDE requires at least 2 weeks to review manuscripts prior to initial journal submission and at least 1 week to review revisions requested by journals (revise and resubmit). While we recognize that extenuating circumstances may occasionally necessitate expedited review, collaborators are expected to plan proactively to ensure sufficient time for the PRIDE team to provide feedback and for collaborators to incorporate that feedback before submission deadlines.
Once the paper has been submitted, please notify our PRIDEnet Clinical Research Coordinator Associate, Ell Hundertmark, so that we can keep track of all paper submissions.

3.2.1 Acknowledgements
It is important to acknowledge the contributions of PRIDE participants in sharing their data with The PRIDE Study, collaborators who may have supported the research but not to the point that would merit authorship, and grant funding supported the research. We provide several boilerplate language below that can be adapted as needed for all paper submissions.

Acknowledgement. The PRIDE Study is a community-engaged research project that serves and is made possible by LGBTQIA+ community involvement at multiple points in the research process, including the dissemination of findings. We acknowledge the courage and dedication of The PRIDE Study participants for sharing their stories; the careful attention of PRIDEnet Participant Advisory Committee (PAC) members for reviewing and improving every study application; and the enthusiastic engagement of PRIDEnet Ambassadors and Community Partners for bringing thoughtful perspectives as well as promoting enrollment and disseminating findings. For more information, please visit https://pridestudy.org/pridenet. In acknowledgment of time spent and expertise provided reviewing research, PAC members are provided a stipend for their time.
Funding. This study was partially funded by the Patient-Centered Outcomes Research Institute (PPRN-1501–26848 to MRL) and the National Institute on Drug Abuse of the National Institutes of Health (K24DA057874 to AF). The statements in this manuscript are solely the responsibility of the authors and do not necessarily represent the views of PCORI, its Board of Governors, or its Methodology Committee and the National Institutes of Health.
Conflict of Interests Disclosure. Dr. Obedin-Maliver has received consultation fees from Ibis Reproductive Health, Hims and Hers Health Inc., Folx Health Inc., Sage Therapeutics and Upstream Inc. on topics unrelated to this work. Dr. Lunn received consultation fees from Hims and Hers Health Inc., Folx Health Inc., Otsuka Pharmaceutical Development and Commercialization Inc., and the American Dental Association on topics unrelated to this work. Dr. Tran received consultation fees from the All of Us Research Program Resources Access Board. No other disclosures were reported.



3.2.2 Code and Data Sharing
Code Sharing. While not required, we encourage investigators to consider sharing the code associated with their study. This is one approach to open science that we believe can improve the rigor and reproducibility of our research. There are several ways to share code:

Supplemental text or PDF file as part of submission
Through PRIDE GitHub and Zenodo accounts
Open Science Framework

Code sharing is increasingly requested by journals; however, the process can vary. The simplest approach may be to provide the code in text or PDF form as part of the supplemental materials with outputs and any identifying information excluded. However, if investigators wish to make their code publicly available and not restricted by journal access, our recommendation is to deposit the code in PRIDE’s GitHub as a repository and link it to our Zenodo account. There are several advantages to this approach:

GitHub allows for clear documentation of what was done in the analysis including all dependencies, versions, and computational environment
Zenodo assigns a Digital Object Identifier to the code that provides a permanent web address that can be referenced
Identifying information (author names, institutions, etc) can be masked for double-blind review
Version tracking that can allow identifying information to be unblinded after paper acceptance while preserving connection to previous versions

For investigators who are relying on our team to conduct analyses internally and are interested in code sharing through PRIDE’s GitHub and Zenodo accounts, we recommend reaching to our biostatistician, Nguyen Tran, who can add the code to GitHub and Zenedo. Once this has been completed, we will provide investigators with a web address and a Digital Object Identifier to a permanent, de-identified snapshot of the code that can be referenced the in paper.
Data Sharing. It is the position of The PRIDE Study to not publicly share datasets generated and/or analyzed for any study due to ethical restrictions related to sensitive participant information. Any investigators interested in conducting research using data The PRIDE Study are welcome to submit a proposal through our Ancillary Study process. We have boilerplate language that can be used as part of the data sharing statement for all paper submissions.

Data Availability Statement. The datasets generated and/or analyzed during the current study are not publicly available due to ethical restrictions related to sensitive participant information but are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request. Researchers interested in The PRIDE Study data may submit a brief application which is reviewed by both a Research Advisory Committee (composed of scientists) and Participant Advisory Committee (composed of participants) to affirm appropriate data use. Details about the Ancillary Study process are available at www.pridestudy.org/collaborate or by contacting support@pridestudy.org or 855-421-9991 (toll-free).

One possible alternative to data sharing of PRIDE Study data is to generate a synthetic dataset with similar characteristics to the original data.1 This can be done using worcs::synthetic() which defaults to a random forest algorithm to generate synthetic data; other models can be specified to generate synthetic values.
iris_syn <- worcs::synthetic(iris)


3.2.3 Preprints
While not required, preprints are one possible options for investigators who are interested in getting their work out sooner. It is important to note that preprints are not peer-reviewed and are potentially subjected to change after going through the journal review process. The general advantage for posting on preprint servers is that these papers will be free for public access, which may be relevant if study findings have time-sensitive policy-related implications, and can be listed on resumes, biosketches, or curriculum vitae for grant applications, job opportunities, or promotion. Commonly used preprint serves include arXiv, medRxiv, PysArXiv, OSF, and Zenodo.


3.2.4 Research Dissemination Back to LGBTQIA+ Communities
In order to “close the loop” and return value to participants that have contributed their data to The PRIDE Study, we require that all investigators who publish their studies using The PRIDE Study data to complete a community-friendly summary of research findings as part of our dissemination guidelines. This requirement reflects our commitment to community-based participatory research and ensures that LGBTQIA+ participants directly benefit from their involvement in the study. The community-friendly summary should be written in accessible, non-technical language that avoids jargon and clearly explains what the research found, why it matters, and how it might impact LGBTQIA+ health and wellbeing. This process is as follows:

Notify The PRIDE Study Team. Upon acceptance of the manuscript, contact our Program Management Director, Daniel Moretti, and PRIDEnet Clinical Research Coordinator Associate, Ell Hundertmark, to notify us of the paper acceptance initiate the research dissemination process.
Complete Community-Friendly Summary. The PRIDE Study team will send a template of the community-friendly summary to the first author for completion within 1 week. Along with the summary, we will request addition information including whether the paper will be open-access, NIH funding support details, anticipated publication date, and final article PDF and citation.  
Collaborative Review and Editing. Our team will review the summary and provide edits as needed to ensure that the language is accessible to participants.
Dissemination. Once the paper is published, the community-friendly summary will be shared with participants through our email listserv and posted on our website. Examples of previous summaries can be found on our website.

title: 3  Publications and Conferences
text:
3.3 Conferences
Any plans to submit abstracts to a conference that uses data from The PRIDE Study are required to be reviewed by The PRIDE Study prior to submission for conferences. The PRIDE Study requests at least 1 week in order to provide feedback. First authors of all abstracts should share their abstracts to all co-authors via email while coping Ell Hundertmark, PRIDEnet Clinical Research Coordinator.
For trainees that are under the mentorship of one of The PRIDE Study co-directors, it is recommended that they reach out to their mentor with ample time in order to discuss funding options for the trip. Stanford Travel policies require that all flights and hotels must be purchased through Stanford Travel.

title: 3  Publications and Conferences
text:
3.4 Resources

Van Lissa CJ, Brandmaier AM, Brinkman L, et al. WORCS: A workflow for open reproducible code in science. Data Science. 2021;4(1):29-49. doi:10.3233/DS-210031
Nowok, B., Raab, G.M and Dibben, C. synthpop: Bespoke creation of synthetic data in R. Journal of Statistical Software. 2016:74(11):1-26. doi:10.18637/jss.v074.i11 .

title: 4  Data
text:
4.1 Data Protection
Protection of LGBTQIA+ participant’s data is a core concern in The PRIDE Study. Working with data in research studies must be grounded in the respect for the people and communities whose information is represented in the data. Thus, we must follow precise and strict guidelines to maintain data privacy and protect sensitive information in The PRIDE Study.

title: 4  Data
text:
4.1.1 Data Security and Storage Guidelines
When new members or trainees join the team, your computer must be encrypted and have institutional security management software installed prior to being granted access to study data. If your computer is not encrypted, please contact your direct supervisor Annesa Flentje, Daniel Morretti Mitchell Lunn, or Juno Obedin-Maliver for support. This is a mandatory prerequisite for all data access privileges.
The PRIDE Study data is stored on secure, HIPAA-compliant servers with restricted access controls. For internal sharing, it recommended that data be shared through Dropbox or Stanford Medicine Box or, if using email, sent through the Secure Email service designated for Moderate or High Risk Data.
For team members who receive access to raw data from The PRIDE Study, it is recommended to never download any data to your personal work device (even if it is encrypted) as this creates unnecessary risk of data breach, loss, or unauthorized access. Instead, store all study data in a secure network drive or institutional cloud storage system, such The PRIDE Study Dropbox for Stanford Box.
When conducting analyses store raw data files only on the approved secure storage system, specify the secure file path in your integrated development environment, and access and analyze data from the secure location without creating local copies. Refer to Section 5.1 for suggested R workflows implementing these practices.
If you need additional guidance on secure data sharing or storage protocols, please contact Nguyen Tran or Mitchell Lunn before handling any study data.


4.1.2 Human Participant Protection
Training. Each internal team member and/or trainee must complete required trainings on the protection of human subjects when working with The PRIDE Study data. Additional trainings may be required depending on the study.
IRB. The PRIDE Study maintains active IRB approval through Stanford University and the Western Institutional Review Board Copernicus Group (WCG) covering all study activities. IRB requirements for Ancillary Studies differ based on whether they involve secondary data analysis of existing PRIDE Study data or primary data collection through novel questionnaires (see Section 5.4 for more details).
In general, Ancillary Studies using only existing PRIDE Study data do not require modifications to the Stanford or WCG IRB approvals. It is cover under the current Stanford and WCG IRB. For Ancillary Studies administrating new questionnaires, The PRIDE Study submits a WCG IRB modification that includes this new questionnaire once it is finalized.
Regardless of the type of Ancillary Study, any external collaborators should consult their home institution(s) regarding local IRB requirements.
Certificate of Confidentiality. The PRIDE Study is protected by a Certificate of Confidentiality from the National Institutes of Health. This certificate helps protect the privacy of research participants by allowing researchers to refuse to disclose identifying information about participants in any civil, criminal, administrative, legislative, or other proceedings, whether at the federal, state, or local level. The Certificate of Confidentiality protects against compulsory legal demands, such as court orders and subpoenas, for identifying information or identifying characteristics of research participants. This protection helps ensure that sensitive information collected during the study, including sexual orientation, gender identity, and health data, remains confidential and cannot be involuntarily disclosed to third parties.

title: 5  Study Design
text:
5.1 PRIDEnet
This section serves as a reference guide for investigators who are preparing a grant application, designing an ancillary study, or drafting a manuscript using data from The PRIDE Study. Key details include description of PRIDEnet (participant-powered research network), The PRIDE Study’s design, metadata outlining collection timepoints, and the Ancillary Study process that can be customized for your specific research needs. These resources are designed to ensure accurate, consistent representation of The PRIDE Study in all research materials.
Established in 2015, PRIDEnet is an extensive network of LGBTQIA+ community partners in the U.S. This national network of community partners is dedicated to catalyzing LGBTQIA+ community involvement at all stages of health research and is built upon well-established community-engaged research principles. Through relationship building and digital communications activities, PRIDEnet engages thousands of LGBTQIA+ individuals across the country and offers multiple low-threshold opportunities to participate in studies and shape research priorities. This is accomplished through a highly active Participant Advisory Committee and a Community Partner Consortium comprising 25 health centers and community organizations across the U.S.

title: 5  Study Design
text:
5.2 THE PRIDE Study
The PRIDE Study is an innovative, community-engaged, longitudinal cohort study of >31,000 LGBTQIA+ adults across the U.S. Launched in 2017 with the support of PRIDEnet, the study employed diverse recruitment strategies including social media campaigns, digital advertisements, and partnerships with LGBTQIA+ community-based organizations and events. Eligible participants were 18 years of age or older, resided in the U.S. or its territories, self-identified as LGBTQIA+, and were able to read and understand English. All participants provided electronic informed consent prior to enrollment.
The PRIDE Study utilizes a state-of-the-art web-based research platform that enables robust participant recruitment, cohort management, real-time cohort statistics, survey administration, deployment of ancillary studies to specific cohort segments, and linkage with external health data sources.
At the study’s inception, participants who enrolled in 2017 were asked to complete an annual health questionnaire that included questions about social experiences and mental and physical health outcomes. Annual questionnaires are reviewed each year by The PRIDE Study team to ensure consistency of core demographic, social, and health questions while updated questions and response options to meet changing clinical criteria, shifts in terminology, evolving community needs, and emerging research priorities identified by our research team and the Participant Advisory Committee. This is further supplemented by a rotating set of periodic content and topics that allow us to collection data on specific health areas over time while balancing the burden of survey completion.
In 2019, The PRIDE Study began implementing a comprehensive lifetime questionnaire that captures participants’ health and experiences across their lifespan. Participants enrolling from 2019 onward are first invited to complete the lifetime questionnaire before proceeding to the annual questionnaire for that administration period. Those who enrolled in 2017 and 2018 were asked to retrospectively complete the lifetime questionnaire when it became available.
The PRIDE Study underwent another modification in 2023 and administered version 2 of the lifetime survey, which revised the survey content by moving specific questions previously found in the annual questionnaire to the lifetime survey. These questions were moved in response to either community feedback or internal assessment that indicated little to no variability in participants’ responses over time.
All participants are invited to complete follow-up annual questionnaires, with each administration period running from May/June through May/June of the following year.

To date, >17,000 participants have completed either version 1 or 2 of the lifetime questionnaire. In addition, >16,000 participants have completed at least one annual questionnaire and >11,000 have completed more than 1 annual questionnaire between 2017-2025.

title: 5  Study Design
text:
5.4 Ancillary Study
Ancillary studies are research investigation conducted in collaboration with The PRIDE Study. The process was developed to encourage research to benefit LGBTQIA+ communities. There are generally 2 type of ancillary studies that can be proposed:

New (primary) data collection: Collection of new data via questionnaire to existing study participants.
Existing (secondary) data analysis: Analysis of existing data from the Annual Questionnaire and/or Lifetime Survey.

All collaborators (internal or external to PRIDE) who are interested in using already collected data from The PRIDE Study or are interested in collecting new data from participants in The PRIDE Study to conduct research focused on LGBTQIA+ populations are required to submit an ancillary study proposal. These proposal are subjected to review from both the research advisory committee and PRIDEnet participant advisory committee as shown in the flowchart below.

Please visit The PRIDE Study website for information on how to get started with the ancillary study process.

title: 5  Study Design
text:
5.5 Resources

Lunn MR, Lubensky M, Hunt C, Flentje A, Capriotti MR, Sooksaman C, Harnett T, Currie D, Neal C, Obedin-Maliver J. A digital health research platform for community engagement, recruitment, and retention of sexual and gender minority adults in a national longitudinal cohort study–The PRIDE Study. J Am Med Inform Assoc. 2019 Aug 1;26(8-9):737-748. doi: 10.1093/jamia/ocz082.
Obedin-Maliver J, Hunt C, Flentje A, Armea-Warren C, Bahati M, Lubensky ME, Dastur Z, Eastburn C, Hundertmark E, Moretti DJ, Pho A, Rescate A, Greene RE, Williams JT, Hursey D, Cook-Daniels L, Lunn MR. Engaging Sexual and Gender Minority (SGM) Communities for Health Research: Building and Sustaining PRIDEnet. J Community Engagem Scholarsh. 2024;16(2):9. doi: 10.54656/jces.v16i2.484.

title: 6  Code Workflow for Reproducible Research
text:
6.1 Code Style and Standard
Coding is difficult! There are many ways to code something that will achieve the same goal. Our general philosophy at PRIDE is based on a general set of coding principles and workflows that are broadly applicable across coding languages and integrated development environments. We recognize the need for transparent and reproducible science while ensuring that we protect the data privacy of LGBTQIA+ participants at The PRIDE Study. We also note that this is a living document: we can update as the team and coding philosophy change over time.
We focus on providing general recommendations and scripts that allow individual members at PRIDE to code how they prefer while maintaining reproducibility, ease of collaboration, and data privacy of participants. We do not tell our team members which language to use or, for the most part, which tools to use within a given language. For those interested in specific recommendations, we suggests reading the works of Van Lissa et al. (2021)1 or the accompanying vignette.
Currently, our team can support workflows in R with more limited support for Python. Thus, we offer guidance specifically in R. We’ll add relevant recommendations if other languages become more prominent in the team
We use R as our primary programming language. It has excellent tools for statistical analysis, visualization, and reproducible research. Here is a brief summary of our approach.
Consistency of code style is essential for collaboration. It speeds up the reading of code and reduces debates on what code should look like. We prefer the Tidyverse style for coding. This can be automatically implemented using the styler package. Despite the name, this guide also applies to non-Tidyverse code, including base R and data.table.
Note that you should run styler interactively in the console, not in one of your R scripts.
Styler also has a helpful add-in for RStudio to let you style the active file, a code selection, etc.
crumbs: 6  Code Workflow for Reproducible Research

title: 6  Code Workflow for Reproducible Research
text:
# style all R-related files in the current directory
styler::style_dir()
crumbs: 6  Code Workflow for Reproducible Research

title: 6  Code Workflow for Reproducible Research
text:
6.2 Naming Things
Naming things is an aspect of code style, but it merits a little extra attention because it is important to readability. In general, strive to make your code self-documenting, e.g., someone else should be able to read your code and understand what it’s doing without talking to you about it. Self-documenting is easier said than done, so code clearness is an essential type of feedback in code review.
Use descriptive names for objects in code, e.g., model_results rather than out or kangaroo_data rather than k. However, do note prefer overly long names (e.g., three words is better than four or five). It’s OK to use terse names where the meaning is generally understood, like n for counts or i and j for loop counters.
Similarly, for functions, describe what the function is doing, preferably in verb format, e.g., simulate_data() is better than f() or data_simulator().
We generally prefer snake_case over camelCase and other naming style conventions unless otherwise appropriate.
crumbs: 6  Code Workflow for Reproducible Research

title: 6  Code Workflow for Reproducible Research
text:
6.3 Project Organization
In general, we recommend using descriptive folder and file names, e.g., R/ or scripts/ to organize source code and output or reports to organize research outputs. You should also include guidance on the organization of your project in a README (see section 6.4.1 for more details about README files).
For example, an R project might look like this:

code/
data/
output/
README.md
my_project.Rproj

The Kiang Lab also has a template for setting new research projects, which includes an appendix that explains the reasoning behind the template.

6.3.1 File Paths
We recommend to not hardcode direct file paths that only exists on your computer:
raw_df = readr::read_csv("/users/ntran/Downloads/data.csv")
Our preference is to use file paths that are relative to the root directory of your project. In RStudio, a root directory is automatically established when you create a new R project. This project-based workflow enables seamless collaboration by allowing team members to access all project data using relative file paths, eliminating the need for hardcoded absolute paths that vary across different users’ systems.
For example, data.csv should be in your project directory (my_project.Rproj), which you can access with relative paths. In addition, we recommend using the here package help specify the relative paths:
raw_df = readr::read_csv(here::here("data", "data.csv"))
The here package can let you refer to files and folders from the root directory of your project no matter where the code exists. For instance, here can be helpful when you have a report in a reports/ folder but want to refer to a data file in the data/ folder. Instead of backtracking ../data/data.csv, you can write here("data", "data.csv").


6.3.2 Documentation
Good documentation practice is essential to ensure that anyone who reviews or inherits your code can quickly understand its purpose and functionality. The primary mode of documenting code is using comments to explain why and not what the code is doing.
The “what” should be evident from well-written, clear code itself; if not, your code may be overly complex. The “why” is often more critical because it provides the reasoning for your analytical decisions, methodological choices, and problem-solving approach. This context cannot always be inferred from the code alone.
# Group together trans and gender diverse identities to avoid small cell sizes
clean_df <- raw_df |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      gi_bin = dplyr::if_else(gender == "cisgender", 0, 1)
    )
In R, the # is used to provide inline comments. We generally recommend putting inline comments go on their own line above the code they describe, not at the end of a line.


6.3.3 README
A README file serves as the primary documentation for your project, explaining its purpose, structure, and how to use it. We recommend including a README file for all research projects to provide collaborators and future users with essential context and guidance. In general, recommended README content should include at least:

Project overview and objectives
Data requirements and sources
Details on installations and setup of any package or version dependencies



6.3.4 Git and GitHub
Our team uses Git and GitHub for managing and hosting version-controlled repositories for our research projects. This effort is led and managed by our biostatistician, Nguyen Tran. For external collaborators whose analyses by our internal team and are interested in making the code publicly available, please contact Nguyen Tran to initiate this process as outlined in Section 3.2.2.
Our team does not require new members or trainees to have prior experience with Git and GitHub. We recognize that these tools may be unfamiliar to many researchers. We encourage developing these skills over time as version control is essential for reproducible research and effective collaboration. Trainees who are interested in learning more about Git and GitHub, Nguyen Tran is available to guide you through the process. This includes helping with creating your personal GitHub account, setting up repositories, learning essential git commands, understanding our project documentation standards, and eventually transferring your work to The PRIDE Study GitHub.
There are also several other labs at Stanford provide introductory guidance to Git and GitHub such as the Kiang Lab and Stanford Health Policy and Data Science. We also recommend the paper by Van Lissa et al. (2021)1 for a coding workflow for reproducible science.






Warning



In general, we urge trainees and collaborators not to upload any data from The PRIDE Study onto GitHub as part of their workflow, whether original or generated during analysis. Making data available through a public GitHub repository is prohibited under our data use agreement and violates our data privacy policies. Even if the repository is private and .gitignore is used to prevent uploading sensitive data to GitHub, this is not foolproof (see UK BioBank data leak).
All data shared by The PRIDE Study must be stored securely (see Section 4.1.1 for examples). If you wish to share data, we recommend either generating a synthetic dataset with similar characteristics to the original data using worcs::synthetic() or using the recommended data availability statement in Section 3.2.2 for your paper submission.




6.3.5 Code Review
Code review is a collaborative practice where team members examine each other’s code before it is finalized or published. This process is essential for ensuring the quality and reproducibility of our research.
Trainees or external collaborators working with PRIDE Study data who would like code review should contact Nguyen Tran in advance to arrange this. Our preference is that code reviews are done through GitHub using a pull request. However, for those not familiar with GitHub, please share your code and documentation through the institution’s secure file sharing platform (Box or Dropbox), along with a brief description of your analysis objectives and any specific areas where you would like feedback.
crumbs: 6  Code Workflow for Reproducible Research

title: 6  Code Workflow for Reproducible Research
text:
6.4 Script Templates for Reproducible Analysis
To support reproducible research practices, we have developed a collection of script templates in R for handling common and repetitive tasks that are prone to human error.
To view scripts aimed at supporting reproducible workflows in R, please check out our repository on GitHub. Please note that these templates may be updated over time based on feedback from team members.
In addition, we recommend the Epidemiologist R Handbook as an R code reference manual that adresses common data, epidemiological, and statistical challenges.
crumbs: 6  Code Workflow for Reproducible Research

title: 6  Code Workflow for Reproducible Research
text:
6.5 Resources

Van Lissa CJ, Brandmaier AM, Brinkman L, et al. WORCS: A workflow for open reproducible code in science. Data Science. 2021;4(1):29-49. doi:10.3233/DS-210031

title: 7  LGBTQIA+ Specific Analytic Considerations
text:
7.1 Measures of Gender, Sex, and Sexuality
LGBTQIA+ identities are multidimensional. This is reflected in how we collect information on different dimensions of sex, gender, and sexuality in The PRIDE Study and the ways in which we analyze these data in our statistical approach. This is grounded in both the methodological literature and the lived experiences of LGBTQIA+ community members of PRIDEnet.
The PRIDE Study collects several measures may be particularly relevant for LGBTQIA+ health research, depending on the specific research question:

title: 7  LGBTQIA+ Specific Analytic Considerations
text:
Variable
Description
Availability




GENDERID_*

Selection of one (or multiple) options that describes current gender identity.
Each gender identity is represented in a separate column.


All annual questionnaires
Lifetime health and experiences survey



SELFBIN_GI

Selection of single option that best describe current gender identity.


All annual questionnaires
Lifetime health and experiences survey



SAAB

Sex assigned at birth, for example on original birth certificate.


All annual questionnaires
Lifetime health and experiences survey



INTERSEX

Self-identification as intersex.


All annual questionnaires
Lifetime health and experiences survey



SAAB_INTERSEX

Sex assigned a birth, for example on original birth certificate, indicate intersex.


2023-2025 annual questionnaires



ORGANS_BORN_*

Selection of one (or multiple) options indicating the organs present at birth.
Each response is represented in a separate column.


2018-2025 annual questionnaires
Lifetime health and experiences survey



ORGANS_NOW_*

Selection of one (or multiple) options indicating the organs currently present.
Each response is represented in a separate column.


2018-2025 annual questionnaires
Lifetime health and experiences survey



ORIENTATION_*

Selection of one (or multiple) options that describes current sexual orientation.
Each sexual orientation is represented in a separate column.


All annual questionnaires
Lifetime health and experiences survey



SELFBIN_SO

Selection of single option that best describe current sexual orientation.


All annual questionnaires
Lifetime health and experiences survey



ATTRACTION_ROM_*

Selection of one (or multiple) options that describes current romantic feelings toward others.
Each romantic attraction is represented in a separate column.


2019-2025 annual questionnaire
Lifetime health and experiences survey



ATTRACTION_SEX_*

Selection of one (or multiple) options that describes current sexual attraction toward others.
Each sexual attraction is represented in a separate column.


2019-2025 annual questionnaire
Lifetime health and experiences survey

title: 7  LGBTQIA+ Specific Analytic Considerations
text:
7.2 Recommended Analytic Strategy
While no single approach fits all research questions, we offer a few key considerations to guide your analytic decisions when working with The PRIDE Study data.
Our approach to including measures of gender, sex, and sexuality balances two essential priorities: representing the diverse identities of PRIDE Study participants while capturing the constructs most relevant to specific research questions. We achieve this balance through a two-part strategy.

7.2.1 Descriptive Analysis
In reporting participant characteristics, we present the full diversity of identities held by participants, honoring the spectrum of gender identities and sexual orientations represented in the LGBTQIA+ community. Oftentimes, this includes reporting all possible responses for current gender identity and current sexual orientation.


7.2.2 Theory-driven Operationalization
For specific research questions, we operationalize measures of gender, sex, and sexuality based on which construct is most relevant to the health outcome being studied. This requires engaging with the existing literature to understand whether gender identity, gender modality, intersex identity, sex assigned at birth, organ-specific status, hormonal status, sexual behavior, sexual orientation identity, enacted gender/sexual orientation stigma or discrimination, or some combination of these factors is most meaningful for the analysis at hand. Oftentimes a multistep approach, such as the validated two-step approach first outlined by Tate et al. (2013)1 for identifying cisgender and transgender people, is needed to accurately identify population of interest or analytic construct of interest.

title: 7  LGBTQIA+ Specific Analytic Considerations
text:
7.3 Resources

Bauer GR, Braimoh J, Scheim AI, Dharma C. Transgender-inclusive measures of sex/gender for population surveys: Mixed-methods evaluation and recommendations. PLoS One. 2017 May 25;12(5):e0178043. doi: 10.1371/journal.pone.0178043.
Bauer GR. Sex and Gender Multidimensionality in Epidemiologic Research. Am J Epidemiol. 2023 Jan 6;192(1):122-132. doi: 10.1093/aje/kwac173.
Restar AJ, Lett E, Menezes NP, Molino AR, Poteat TC, Dean LT, Glick JL, Baker KE, Cole SW. Getting precise about gender and sex measurement: a primer for epidemiologists. Am J Epidemiol. 2024 Dec 2;193(12):1861-1867. doi: 10.1093/aje/kwae144.
Suen LW, Lunn MR, Katuzny K, Finn S, Duncan L, Sevelius J, Flentje A, Capriotti MR, Lubensky ME, Hunt C, Weber S, Bibbins-Domingo K, Obedin-Maliver J. What Sexual and Gender Minority People Want Researchers to Know About Sexual Orientation and Gender Identity Questions: A Qualitative Study. Arch Sex Behav. 2020 Oct;49(7):2301-2318. doi: 10.1007/s10508-020-01810-y.
Tate CC, Ledbetter JN, Youssef CP. A two-question method for assessing gender categories in the social and medical sciences. J Sex Res. 2013;50(8):767-76. doi: 10.1080/00224499.2012.690110.
Tordoff DM, Minalga B, Catháin NÓ, Fernandez A, Gross B, Glick SN; The Seattle Trans and Nonbinary Sexual Health (STARS) Advisory Board. Comparing two-step approaches to measuring gender identity: the reliability and applications of asking about sex assigned at birth vs transgender self-identification. Am J Epidemiol. 2025 May 7;194(5):1255-1263. doi: 10.1093/aje/kwae341.

title: 8  Contact
text:
We are a small but mighty team dedicated to supporting investigators using The PRIDE Study data! We strive to provide responsive assistance throughout your research journey, from initial data requests to manuscript preparation and community dissemination. Please do not hesitate to reach out with questions. We are here to help ensure your research is successful and benefits the LGBTQIA+ communities we serve. Below are key team members and their areas of expertise to help direct your inquiries:
If you are a new investigator and interested in collaborating with The PRIDE Study, please reach us at: support@pridestudy.org. For specific inquiries, please review the information below for the relevant contact and their contact information.

Requires specific information about The PRIDE Study, Ancillary Study Inquiry, Trainees under direct supervision, Grant application
